content.text: Partitioning & File Layout in Python Data Pipelines

Organizing data on disk is one of the most consequential architectural decisions in a data pipeline. How files are named, grouped, and structured on a filesystem or object store directly determines query speed, storage cost, pipeline maintainability, and the ability to scale. A well-partitioned dataset can turn a 10-minute full scan into a 2-second targeted read. A poorly laid out one can make even simple aggregations painfully slow.
is the practice of dividing a large dataset into smaller, physically separated chunks based on the values of one or more columns — called
. Instead of storing all data in a single monolithic file, data is split across directories (or object-store prefixes) where each directory corresponds to a specific partition value.
refers to the broader set of decisions about how files are organized: the directory hierarchy, file naming conventions, file formats, file sizes, and compression choices.
Together, partitioning and file layout form the
of a data pipeline — the bridge between raw ingestion and efficient downstream consumption.
Without intentional partitioning, every query must scan the entire dataset, even if it only needs 0.1% of the data. This is called a
, and it is expensive in time, compute, and money.

Consider a dataset of 500 GB of web server logs. A query asking for "all errors on 2024-06-01" without partitioning reads all 500 GB. With date-based partitioning, it reads only the ~1.4 GB directory for that day. That is a 350× reduction in I/O.
Modern query engines (Spark, DuckDB, Trino, Athena) natively exploit partition structures through
Cloud object stores (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) charge per byte read. Scanning less data directly reduces cost.
Pipelines that write incrementally need a layout that supports
When a query engine reads a partitioned dataset and the
clause filters on a partition key, the engine inspects directory names and skips any partition that cannot contain matching rows. This is called
predicate pushdown at the partition level
. No file in a pruned partition is opened or read.

dataset/
├── year=2024/
│   ├── month=01/
│   │   ├── part-0001.parquet
│   │   └── part-0002.parquet
│   └── month=06/
│       ├── part-0001.parquet
│       └── part-0002.parquet
. Everything else is skipped at the filesystem level — before any data is read.
The most widely adopted convention is
, which encodes partition key-value pairs directly in directory names using the
format. This format originated in Apache Hive but is now a universal standard recognized by PyArrow, Pandas, Spark, DuckDB, Trino, Athena, BigQuery, and virtually every modern query engine.

s3://my-bucket/events/
    country=US/
        year=2024/
            month=06/
                part-00001.parquet
    country=DE/
        year=2024/
            month=06/
                part-00001.parquet
tells any Hive-compatible reader that all rows in that directory have
, so when reading the dataset, the engine can automatically populate the
column from the path rather than requiring it to be stored in every row.
The choice of file format is inseparable from partitioning strategy because it determines compression ratios, read patterns, and compatibility.
The dominant format for analytical workloads. Parquet is a
— data is stored column by column rather than row by row. This means:
Reading only specific columns is fast because non-selected columns are not read from disk at all.
Compression is highly effective because similar values are stored together (e.g., all values of a
column are adjacent, making run-length encoding very efficient).
Metadata is stored in the file footer, allowing readers to inspect schema and row group statistics without reading data blocks.
(chunks of rows, typically 128 MB each) and within each row group, each column is stored as a
. Each column chunk stores min/max statistics that enable
, the engine skips that row group entirely.

import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.parquet as pq

table = pa.table({
    "event_date": ["2024-06-01", "2024-06-01", "2024-06-02"],
    "country": ["US", "DE", "US"],
    "revenue": [100.0, 200.0, 150.0],
})

# Write with Hive-style partitioning
pq.write_to_dataset(
    table,
    root_path="output/events",
    partition_cols=["event_date", "country"],
    compression="snappy",
)

output/events/
    event_date=2024-06-01/
        country=US/
            part-0.parquet
        country=DE/
            part-0.parquet
    event_date=2024-06-02/
        country=US/
            part-0.parquet

Apache ORC (Optimized Row Columnar) is another columnar format, most common in Hive and Hadoop ecosystems. It has similar properties to Parquet but uses different internal compression and indexing. Parquet is generally preferred in Python-centric pipelines due to stronger PyArrow and Pandas support.

binary format with schema embedded in the file header. Avro is well-suited for streaming (Kafka) and write-heavy workloads where you need full rows frequently. It does not provide columnar read efficiency. Typically used for raw ingestion landing zones, not for analytical query layers.

Plain text formats. Useful for interoperability and human readability but extremely inefficient for large-scale analytics: no columnar access, no embedded statistics, no compression at the file level (unless externally compressed), and no schema enforcement. Appropriate for small files, external delivery formats, or pipeline entry points — not for internal storage in a data pipeline.
How It Works — End to End
Partition keys are the columns that will determine directory structure. The right keys depend entirely on how the data will be queried.
when there are 50 million users creates 50 million directories — each containing a few bytes. This is called the
column splits data into only two directories, providing minimal pruning benefit.
If data arrives hourly, partition by
so each pipeline run writes to exactly one partition.
Partition key order matters. More selective (higher-level) keys should come first. The most common temporal hierarchy is:
For event-driven pipelines adding a business dimension:
The rule: put the dimension you filter on
highest in the tree, because the engine prunes entire subtrees based on the first matching partition level.
File size within each partition is as important as the partition structure itself. Both extremes cause problems:
Too many small files (< 10 MB each):
Every file requires a separate filesystem or object-store API call to open.
Query engines spend more time on metadata operations than actual data reading.
Object stores like S3 charge per API request, so thousands of tiny files increase cost.
— a well-known bottleneck in Hadoop and Spark ecosystems.
Files that are too large (> 1–2 GB each):
Cannot be read in parallel by multiple workers without splitting.
Re-writing a single bad record requires rewriting the entire file.
For streaming append workloads, waiting for a file to be "large enough" before writing introduces latency.
Target file size: 128 MB to 512 MB per Parquet file.
This aligns with HDFS block size conventions and gives modern query engines optimal parallelism.
Step 4: Manage Partition Evolution Over Time
Partitions accumulate over time. A daily pipeline running for 2 years produces 730 partition directories. This is fine. But certain operations require care:
Adding data to an existing partition
— append a new file inside the existing partition directory, or rewrite the partition. Never modify files in-place in Parquet; the format is immutable.
— Parquet supports schema evolution (adding columns), but existing files will return
for new columns. The reader must handle this gracefully.
— must rewrite the affected partition files entirely, since Parquet files are immutable. Table formats like Delta Lake or Apache Iceberg solve this with transaction logs.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      Raw Ingestion Layer                            │
│   (CSV, JSON, Avro from APIs, Kafka, databases)                     │
└──────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                               │
                               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   Transformation & Partitioning                     │
│                                                                     │
│   1. Parse & validate incoming data                                 │
│   2. Assign partition key values (e.g., extract date from timestamp)│
│   3. Write to Hive-style partitioned Parquet on S3/GCS/local       │
│   4. Compact small files if needed (optional compaction job)        │
└──────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                               │
                               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              Partitioned Data Lake / Storage Layer                  │
│                                                                     │
│   s3://bucket/events/                                               │
│       year=2024/                                                    │
│           month=06/                                                 │
│               day=01/   ← partition directory                       │
│                   part-0001.parquet  (128–512 MB each)             │
│                   part-0002.parquet                                 │
│               day=02/                                               │
│                   part-0001.parquet                                 │
└──────────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                               │
                               ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   Query / Consumption Layer                         │
│                                                                     │
│   DuckDB, Trino, Athena, Spark, Pandas + PyArrow                   │
│   → Partition pruning skips irrelevant directories                 │
│   → Predicate pushdown skips irrelevant row groups within files     │
│   → Columnar reads return only requested columns                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.parquet as pq
import pyarrow.dataset as ds
from datetime import date

# Simulate a batch of data arriving from an API
records = [
    {"event_date": "2024-06-01", "country": "US", "event": "purchase", "amount": 49.99},
    {"event_date": "2024-06-01", "country": "DE", "event": "view",     "amount": 0.0},
    {"event_date": "2024-06-02", "country": "US", "event": "purchase", "amount": 120.0},
    {"event_date": "2024-06-02", "country": "US", "event": "refund",   "amount": -49.99},
]

schema = pa.schema([
    pa.field("event_date", pa.string()),
    pa.field("country",    pa.string()),
    pa.field("event",      pa.string()),
    pa.field("amount",     pa.float64()),
])

table = pa.Table.from_pylist(records, schema=schema)

# Write with Hive-style partitioning
pq.write_to_dataset(
    table,
    root_path="data/events",
    partition_cols=["event_date", "country"],
    compression="snappy",              # fast compression, good ratio
    existing_data_behavior="overwrite_or_ignore",  # safe re-runs
)

data/events/
    event_date=2024-06-01/
        country=US/
            part-0.parquet
        country=DE/
            part-0.parquet
    event_date=2024-06-02/
        country=US/
            part-0.parquet

import pyarrow.dataset as ds

# Open the entire dataset — no data is read yet
dataset = ds.dataset("data/events", format="parquet", partitioning="hive")

# Apply a filter — only files under event_date=2024-06-01/country=US/ are read
table = dataset.to_table(
    filter=(
        (ds.field("event_date") == "2024-06-01") &
        (ds.field("country") == "US")
    ),
    columns=["event", "amount"]   # only read needed columns
)

print(table.to_pandas())

argument tells PyArrow to parse directory names as column values. The filter is applied both at the partition level (skipping entire directories) and within files (using Parquet statistics to skip row groups).

import pandas as pd

# Pandas uses PyArrow under the hood for Parquet
df = pd.read_parquet(
    "data/events",
    filters=[
        ("event_date", "==", "2024-06-01"),
        ("country", "==", "US"),
    ],
    columns=["event", "amount"],
)
print(df)
parameter uses PyArrow's DNF (Disjunctive Normal Form) filter syntax. Partition pruning and row-group skipping both happen automatically.
In a real pipeline, data arrives continuously. The pattern is to write each batch to the correct partition:

import pyarrow as pa
import pyarrow.parquet as pq
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def write_batch(records: list[dict], root_path: str) -> None:
    """
    Write a batch of records to a partitioned Parquet dataset.
    Each record must have 'event_date' and 'country' fields.
    """
    if not records:
        return

    table = pa.Table.from_pylist(records)

    pq.write_to_dataset(
        table,
        root_path=root_path,
        partition_cols=["event_date", "country"],
        compression="snappy",
        basename_template=f"part-{{i}}-{datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime('%Y%m%d%H%M%S')}.parquet",
        existing_data_behavior="overwrite_or_ignore",
    )

# Simulate hourly pipeline run
batch = [
    {"event_date": "2024-06-03", "country": "US", "event": "login", "amount": 0.0},
    {"event_date": "2024-06-03", "country": "CA", "event": "purchase", "amount": 88.0},
]
write_batch(batch, root_path="data/events")
with a timestamp in the filename prevents collisions when multiple pipeline runs write to the same partition concurrently.
DuckDB can query Hive-partitioned Parquet files directly with full partition pruning:

import duckdb

con = duckdb.connect()

# DuckDB auto-discovers Hive partitions via glob
result = con.execute("""
    SELECT
        country,
        SUM(amount) AS total_revenue,
        COUNT(*) AS event_count
    FROM read_parquet(
        'data/events/**/*.parquet',
        hive_partitioning = true
    )
    WHERE event_date = '2024-06-01'
      AND event = 'purchase'
    GROUP BY country
    ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
""").fetchdf()

print(result)
subdirectories and skips all others — even though the glob
would match every file, partition pruning prevents them from being opened.
Over time, many small files accumulate in frequently written partitions. Compaction merges them into larger files:

import pyarrow.dataset as ds
import pyarrow.parquet as pq

def compact_partition(partition_path: str, target_row_group_size: int = 128 * 1024 * 1024) -> None:
    """
    Read all small files in a partition directory and rewrite as a single
    optimally-sized Parquet file.
    """
    dataset = ds.dataset(partition_path, format="parquet")
    table = dataset.to_table()

    # Overwrite with a single file
    output_path = f"{partition_path}/compacted.parquet"
    pq.write_table(
        table,
        output_path,
        compression="snappy",
        row_group_size=target_row_group_size,
    )

    # In production: delete old files after confirming the write succeeded
    # and update any catalog (e.g., Glue, Iceberg metadata) accordingly

compact_partition("data/events/event_date=2024-06-01/country=US")
An e-commerce platform processes 50 million events per day across 200 countries. Events include page views, product clicks, purchases, and refunds.
"Hourly purchase funnel for a specific date"
"All refund events for a specific order ID in the last 7 days"

s3://events-lake/raw/
    event_type=purchase/
        year=2024/month=06/day=01/hour=00/
            part-00001.parquet   (~200 MB)
            part-00002.parquet   (~200 MB)
        year=2024/month=06/day=01/hour=01/
            ...
    event_type=view/
        year=2024/month=06/day=01/hour=00/
            part-00001.parquet   (~500 MB)
            ...
    event_type=refund/
        year=2024/month=06/day=01/hour=00/
            part-00001.parquet   (~20 MB)

is the top-level partition because queries almost always filter by event type first. Within each type, data is partitioned by year/month/day/hour, matching the hourly pipeline cadence. This means each pipeline run writes to exactly one
directory, making the write pattern clean and idempotent.
For the "revenue by country" query, the engine prunes to
and reads all hour directories for that day — roughly 16 files out of thousands. The
column is stored inside the Parquet files (not as a partition key) because 200 countries × 24 hours × 365 days would create 1.7 million directories — the small files problem at scale.

as partition keys creates millions of directories, each containing a few kilobytes of data. Opening millions of files is slower than reading one large file. The rule: partition keys should have low-to-medium cardinality (dates, countries, status codes, event types — not IDs).

Partitioning by year/month/day/hour/minute creates 525,600 directories per year. For most workloads, hour-level granularity is sufficient. Finer granularity means more directories and smaller files, hurting performance.
Storing 2 TB in a single directory with thousands of files provides no partition pruning and forces full scans of all files.
Mismatched partition cadence and write cadence.

If data arrives hourly but partitions are daily, every hourly pipeline run appends a new small file to the daily partition. After 24 runs, there are 24 small files per day. This requires a separate compaction job to merge them.
in others breaks partition detection in query engines that compare directory name strings. Always normalize partition key values before writing.
Parquet files are immutable. Never append to an existing Parquet file. To add rows to a partition, write a new file alongside existing ones, or rewrite the entire partition.

Writing millions of 1 KB files (common in streaming pipelines with micro-batches) causes catastrophic performance degradation. Always implement a compaction strategy or use buffering at the write layer.
Within each partition file, the order of rows affects compression and row-group skipping efficiency. Sorting by high-cardinality columns used in point lookups (e.g.,
) before writing allows Parquet's min/max statistics to enable fine-grained row-group skipping.
Match partition keys to your most common query filters.
Audit actual query patterns before choosing partition keys. The goal is for the most common
clauses to hit as few partitions as possible.
Aim for 128 MB–512 MB per Parquet file.
This is the sweet spot for parallelism and I/O efficiency. Implement compaction jobs to merge small files when they accumulate.
Use Snappy compression as the default.
Snappy provides fast compression and decompression with reasonable compression ratios. For cold/archival data where read speed matters less, use Zstandard (zstd) for better compression ratios.
Sort data within partitions by commonly filtered non-partition columns.
within a date partition, sort rows by
before writing. Parquet's row-group statistics then enable efficient skipping.
Each pipeline run should produce the same output given the same input. Use
or write to a staging path then atomically rename. This enables safe retries.

Register partition metadata in AWS Glue, Apache Hive Metastore, or Unity Catalog rather than relying on filesystem discovery. Catalog-based partition discovery is much faster than listing directories for large datasets.

Recent partitions (last 7–30 days) are queried frequently. Older partitions are rarely accessed. Use storage tiering (e.g., S3 Standard → S3 Glacier) or separate storage classes to reduce cost on cold data.
Consider table formats for mutable data.

If data needs updates or deletes (GDPR compliance, error corrections), use Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, or Apache Hudi instead of raw Parquet. These formats add a transaction log on top of Parquet files, enabling ACID transactions, schema evolution, and time travel without rewriting entire datasets.
The speedup from partition pruning is proportional to the fraction of data excluded. If 1% of partitions are read, the query is up to 100× faster than a full scan — assuming I/O is the bottleneck.

Query engines evaluate partition filters before opening any files. With Hive-style directories, this happens by listing directory names and comparing them to the filter expression. With table formats (Iceberg, Delta), this happens by reading a small transaction log or manifest file — which is faster than directory listing at scale.
The Small Files Problem at Scale

On object stores like S3, each file open requires an HTTP request. A dataset with 1 million 1 KB files requires 1 million HTTP requests to read, even though the total data is only 1 GB. At S3 request pricing of $0.0004 per 1,000 requests, that is $0.40 per full scan — and the latency of 1 million serial requests is unbearable.
The solution is compaction: periodically merge small files into larger ones. This can be done as a separate offline job or triggered automatically by table formats.

Parquet files are the unit of parallelism for most query engines. A Spark job with 100 executor cores reading a partition with 10 files cannot use more than 10 cores for that partition (one file per task). More files (up to the core count) means more parallelism. Fewer, larger files mean better compression and less metadata overhead but less parallelism. The 128 MB target balances both.
Row Group Statistics and Data Skipping

Within a Parquet file, each row group stores min/max statistics per column. A query engine can skip a row group entirely if the filter value falls outside the min/max range. For this to be effective, rows must be
by the filtered column within the file. A randomly ordered file has min=min_value and max=max_value for every row group, making statistics useless.
Sorting by the most selectively filtered column within each partition (known as
in Delta Lake/Iceberg) dramatically improves data skipping efficiency.
Core library for reading/writing Parquet and dataset partitioning in Python
High-level DataFrame API; uses PyArrow for Parquet I/O
Embedded analytical SQL engine; native Hive partition support
Distributed engine; native partitioned Parquet read/write
ACID table format on top of Parquet; managed via
Table format with rich partition evolution and time travel; integrated with Spark, Trino, Flink
Table format focused on upserts and streaming ingestion
Serverless catalog and query engine; fully Hive-partition aware
Orchestration for scheduling pipeline runs and compaction jobs
Filesystem abstraction allowing PyArrow to read from S3, GCS, Azure transparently
Batch pipelines, data lakes, read-heavy analytics
No ACID; schema changes require care; no deletes
Needs ACID, upserts, deletes, or time travel
Large-scale pipelines, partition evolution, multiple engines
No columnar reads, poor compression, no statistics
Transactional workloads, small data, complex joins
Poor scalability for large analytical scans
When partition key values are derived from user input or external data, an attacker could inject path separators (
) as partition values, causing writes to unintended locations. Always sanitize partition key values before using them in file paths.
Writing directly to the final destination risks partial files being visible to readers during a write failure. The safe pattern is write-to-temp-then-rename:

import os
import shutil

tmp_path = "data/events/_staging/batch-001.parquet"
final_path = "data/events/event_date=2024-06-01/country=US/batch-001.parquet"

# Write to staging
pq.write_table(table, tmp_path)

# Atomic move to final location
os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(final_path), exist_ok=True)
shutil.move(tmp_path, final_path)
On cloud object stores, this is achieved by writing to a
prefix and then using the object store's copy-then-delete API, since true atomic rename is not available on S3.
Access control at the partition level.
Cloud object stores support IAM policies at the prefix level. Sensitive partitions (e.g.,
) can be restricted to specific roles while other partitions remain broadly accessible. This is a significant advantage of file-based partitioning over database row-level security.

Compaction jobs that delete original files before confirming the compacted file is complete and correct risk data loss. Always verify the compacted file's row count matches the sum of source files before deleting originals.

Partitioning splits a dataset into directories by column values, enabling query engines to skip irrelevant data entirely through partition pruning — the single most impactful optimization for large-scale analytical reads.
directory naming is the universal standard, recognized by every major query engine and Python library.
Parquet is the dominant format for analytical pipelines: columnar, compressed, with internal statistics that enable row-group skipping beyond partition pruning.
Partition key cardinality must be low-to-medium — never partition on IDs or other high-cardinality fields, as this creates millions of tiny files that destroy performance.
File sizes of 128 MB–512 MB per Parquet file are the target; compaction jobs are a necessary operational component of any pipeline that writes small incremental batches.
PyArrow, DuckDB, and pandas provide Python-native APIs for reading and writing Hive-partitioned Parquet datasets with full partition pruning and predicate pushdown.
For workloads requiring updates, deletes, or ACID guarantees, Delta Lake and Apache Iceberg add a transaction layer on top of partitioned Parquet without abandoning the file-layout model.
Sorting data within partitions by frequently filtered non-partition columns amplifies Parquet's internal statistics, enabling data skipping at the row-group level in addition to the partition level.
